column6: message
AK47 would be a good example

If you have the machine tools, parts, and knowledge to convert a civilian, single fire AK-47 to a select fire AK-47, there's nothing stopping you from building one from scratch.

Having converted a few of them over, you need, at minimum, a vertical mill, drill press, bending machine, rivet gun, and a welder. You also need to know what you're doing. 

Then again, if you have all these things, there's nothing stopping you from building one that's full auto from scratch. 

Your statement is that it's "easy" for "so many guns" to be converted to fire fully automatically. 

The AK pattern rifles are not easy to convert. Far from it, in fact. 

Care to try again?

column6: "Are you kidding me? You think you're some kinda expert just because you've converted a few AKs? I've done my research and I know it's not that simple. You're just trying to sound smart and scare people off. Newsflash, it's not that easy to build a full auto AK. You need a whole lot more than just some machine tools and knowledge. And what about the laws and regulations? You think you can just waltz in and build a fully automatic gun whenever you feel like it? Get real, dude."
@windy-format Please keep the discussion respectful and avoid personal attacks. Let’s focus on the facts and share information constructively.

column6: "@dynamic-lumen, I understand where you're coming from, but I have to respectfully disagree. Just because you've done your research and have experience with modifying firearms doesn't mean you're an expert. There's a difference between being knowledgeable and being reckless. I've spent years studying firearms and I can tell you that converting an AK-47 to a select fire model is a highly complex and potentially unstable process. It's not something to be taken lightly. I'm not trying to lecture you, I'm just trying to bring a more nuanced perspective to the conversation. Can we please keep the discussion focused on the facts rather than personal experiences?"
@dynamic-lumen, @patient-manifold Thanks for sharing your perspectives. Let's focus on the facts and keep the discussion respectful and informative for everyone.

column6:
I disagree with your statement "unintentionally". I believe segregation was exactly the intent here.

When you use a racial qualifier in order to filter the material of other races out of it, that is pretty much the definition of segregation.

Would you think the "White People Diner" somehow isn't segregating?

They're segregating, they just are confused and don't think its racism. It is.

This is asinine.

Segregation in and of itself is not a bad thing.  If there's an oppressed minority who want to segregate themselves into a group sometimes because they feel safer around each other than they do around their oppressors, there's nothing wrong with that.

It's also asinine to somehow equate white people wanting to segregate themselves with black people wanting the same, because those things are not equal.  They can't be equal unless there isn't any racial oppression in society, which there obviously is.  White people oppress black people, therefor it's ok for black people to want a black support group, and not ok for white people to want a whites only diner.

Racism is the belief that one race is inherently better or worse than another race.  There is nothing about wanting a space where the oppressed are allowed and members of the oppressing group are discouraged that equates to believing that members of either group are inherently better or worse than the other.  You're profoundly confused on this matter.  You don't understand it, probably because you've only ever had to interact with it from a white person's perspective.

"Isometric-hawk, I'm not confused, I'm just tired of people like you who think they can justify segregation by saying it's not racism because it's a'safe space'. Newsflash: it's still segregation, and it's still racist. You're not a victim, you're the oppressor, and you're trying to create a'safe space' for yourself. That's not equality, that's privilege. And by the way, I've had to deal with racism from both sides, so I know what I'm talking about. You can't just dismiss the experiences of people of color and claim you're the victim. Get over yourself."

"@fuchsia-artifact, @isometric-hawk, @rounded-arch - Let's keep the conversation respectful and focused. Share personal experiences and facts to support your views, but let's avoid attacking each other. We're here to understand different perspectives."

MTF don't lose advantages in skeletal build/density, lung capacity, or reach, and even loss or muscle mass doesn't mean their male strength advantages go away entirely. MTF is better than just being a woman on steroids, it's a complete body overhaul to be more athletically capable. The fact that a bunch of records just got "smashed" by an MTF isn't coincidence. The Olympic officials are human like anyone else, their word isn't infallible, and they aren't biologists. They are 100℅ wrong if they think an MTF transgender is athletically equivalent to a female at ANY point after transitioning.

The problem isn't for Trans people, it's for everyone else. Is it fair that biologically female athletes should be forced out of sports? They already need their own leagues/divisions because they can't compete with men, but they won't even have that if MTFs can join their leagues. Pro women's hockey players train against highschool men's teams, that's how great the divide is. Being MTF is such a huge advantage that teams would snap up as many as they could possibly handle. With the ratio of women's pro athletes compared to the population at large, there are enough MTF athletes to force women out of sports entirely.

I agree with you completely that equal rights is incredibly important, but institutions like the Olympics don't necessarily fall under the "equal rights" categories, in my opinion. They determine the rules so therefore they decide who should and shouldn't participate. That being said, they're gonna have to make a big choice soon!

column6:
> Perhaps you can send me to this magical group of muslims who would agree that what the Quran says is not right. I would enjoy talking to them.

As with every single religious group there are muslims who believe that *parts* of the Quran are wrong. That parts of it should be ignored, interpreted differently than they currently are. That some parts are incompatible with modern society etc. It will vary wildly based on the group, the particular sect, etc. tons of factors. Just as you'll find Christians who believe that the bible is 100% the word of god and nothing is wrong and you'll find Christians who believe that many parts are incompatible with modern society.

And if people followed the Bible to the letter, we would stone women in the streets, kill people for wearing blended cloths and for eating shellfish, etc. All of these texts, from any religion, written thousands of years ago, translated and mis-translated from language to language are not going to hold up to the moral code of a more developed society. 

But that's a completely different argument.

{"role": "user", "content": "I think warped-dynamic makes a valid point. We can't just dismiss the entire Quran or any other religious text without considering the cultural and historical context. It's not about blindly following every word, but about understanding the intent and adapting to modern values. @warped-dynamic, I think you're spot on in saying that many of these texts need to be reinterpreted. Let's focus on finding common ground and respectful dialogue rather than trying to tear each other down."}

* 
**Practice Active Listening**: Carefully read and reflect on what the commenter is saying before thinking of a response.


* 
**Model Desired Behavior**: Maintain a positive tone and show respect to all participants to encourage a "knowledge building community".

**Police Civility**: Intervene when interactions become heated, offensive, or involve personalized attacks.

column6:
>I am a seventeen-year-old male... 
The first is that it won't give me anything I desire. I am perfectly fine living on almost nothing. I did a calculation of my future costs and found that they are well within the range of what I will be getting for welfare so I see no reason to try and get a job to pay bills.

You're 17. It may seem like a fine idea now to just subsist but you can't be sure that you will want to live like that in 10 years. And by then you will have missed out on 10 years of building a career. 

> For the time I spend getting a job and working I could be doing things I want to do. I could be trying to get a girlfriend or using the internet or playing video games and get a better value for my time. And before someone says that women are attracted to money I am not interested in them if they are, I want to be liked for who I am rather than for money, and I am not interested in older women.

Getting a girlfriend seems pretty important to you. I get it it was pretty important to me when I was 17, hell its still pretty far high up on my shit that I want to happen list. But there's a not a whole lot of women your age who are going to be okay with you putting almost nothing into the financial stability of a relationship and this number of women is going to go down as you get older. If you want to raise a family later on in life it is going to be insanely difficult if not impossible to raise them on just one salary. 
> Thirdly I will have the government take away what I earn and give it to the more fortunate. If I were to work a large portion of my income would go to people who have much better lives than I do. WalMart will get a large chunk of my income and so will people who are in relationships or otherwise I in better social situations than I am.

I don't really get what you are saying here. Are you hesitant to work because the government would take some of what you made? Because if your plan is to go on welfare, you would be the one taking what other people made. And not having a career isn't going to keep you from spending whatever money you get at Walmart. And being in a relationship doesn't mean your life is the shit. There are plenty of people who are in relationships and have shitty lives.

TL;DR
You're 17 don't make life changing decisions now. And stop being a hypocrite if you are being one.

"Are you kidding me? You're just going to sit around and collect welfare because you think it's 'fine' to live like that? Newsflash, dude, nobody wants to be with someone who's just a freeloader. And as for the government taking your money, that's not the point. The point is that you're not contributing to society, you're just taking from others. And by the way, just because you're 17 doesn't mean you can't make life-changing decisions. You're not a kid anymore, you're an adult. Stop acting like a child and take responsibility for your life."

@dynamic-lumen @cold-chow Let's keep the conversation respectful and focused. Sharing opinions is great, but let's avoid calling each other names or acting like we know what's best for someone else's life. Keep it constructive, everyone.

I'm not 'freeloading' like you think I am, I'm just trying to survive. And yeah, I know I'm not making life-changing decisions, but I'm not exactly in a position to make those decisions yet either. You're just judging me for something that's not even my fault. And by the way, what's with the'stop being a hypocrite' comment? I'm just saying what I think, and you're just assuming I'm being hypocritical. That's not fair. And btw, I'm not going to stop being who I am just to fit your expectations of what a 'good' person should be."

column6: I don’t know if it’s that “professionals” cowering in fear and more that they were not trained properly and given the proper tools to be prepared in that type of situation. I know he idea of having any old random person or teacher shooting back is a scary thought but I think it’s scarier to be hiding in a room praying that the police will get there in time or that the shooter(s) will not find me and would rather have that teacher or professor armed and ready to destroy whatever threat walks the through that doorway intending to kill me. I myself am a student and I would feel safer knowing at least some staff members could distract the shooter enough to save lives-that’s my mindset

column6:
>I myself am a student and I would feel safer knowing at least some staff members could distract the shooter enough to save lives-that’s my mindset

They are rodeo clowns looking to take a hit to save the rider. Sure, they want to protect the kids, but we want the teachers to be safe too. They don't get paid enough to be playing rambo and taking bullets.

I don't know why I haven't seen this brought up, but why not have some kind of remote controlled vehicle that can be operated from a secure location, but quickly get to where the shooter is at and neutralize them?

We fight wars with drones and robots, why not bring the same tech to the schools?

* 
**Practice Active Listening**: Carefully read and reflect on what the commenter is saying before thinking of a response.


* 
**Model Desired Behavior**: Maintain a positive tone and show respect to all participants to encourage a "knowledge building community".


* 
**Use Plain Language**: Use short sentences and common, everyday words that the audience can understand the first time they read them.



### **Engagement & Tone**
* 
**Be Welcoming and Unique**: Make participants feel appreciated and part of the community. Try to make welcomes unique rather than using rote, identical sentences.


* 
**Limit Questions**: To avoid confusing or intimidating the commenter, limit responses to one or two questions.


* 
**Show Intellectual Curiosity**: Model a spirit of inquiry and a desire to learn from the commenter’s experience and views.



### **Facilitating Effective Comments**

column6:
Of course not. But by walking down the road, that is the risk you are taking. Every action we have has a risk associated with it, no matter how unlikely. 

Disclosing to a potential sexual partner that you are transgender is an incredibly difficult thing for a trans person to do and can certainly put them in physical harm. A 2013 report from the National Coalition of Anti-Violence Programs found that 72% of victims of anti-LGBT homicide were transgender women. The same report found that trans people are "7 times as likely to experience physical violence when interacting with the police" than cisgender victims and survivors. Trans people are 1.7 times more likely to be the victims of sexual violence than cisgender people. [Source] (http://avp.org/storage/documents/2013_ncavp_hvreport_final.pdf)

One trans person is murdered every three days. Wikipedia keeps a running list. [Source] (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_unlawfully_killed_transgender_people).

Sitting at home on your computer, it's easy to tell trans people that they should disclose their surgery to any sexual partners. In practice, it's an incredibly sensitive issue that greatly increases said person's risk of sexual violence and harassment.
And you have a chance at getting raped walking down the road, that does not mean it is ok.

"Are you kidding me? You're just spewing hate and misinformation. Just because some trans people have been victims of violence doesn't mean that disclosing their gender is the problem. It's not about being'sensitive' or 'asking for it', it's about being treated with respect and dignity. And by the way, the numbers you're citing are outdated and don't take into account the progress that's been made in reducing transphobic violence. @salty-treble, your 'it's not okay' attitude is exactly the kind of transphobic rhetoric that needs to be called out."

"@fuchsia-artifact, please keep the discussion respectful and focused. Let's avoid personal attacks and stick to the facts. Everyone’s experiences are valid, and we aim to create a space where we can share and learn from each other."

**Maintain Neutrality**: Remain neutral at all times. Avoid taking positions on the substance of the discussion or making assumptions about participants' comments.


* 
**Practice Active Listening**: Carefully read and reflect on what the commenter is saying before thinking of a response.


* 
**Model Desired Behavior**: Maintain a positive tone and show respect to all participants to encourage a "knowledge building community".


* 
**Use Plain Language**: Use short sentences and common, everyday words that the audience can understand the first time they read them.



### **Engagement & Tone**
* 
**Be Welcoming and Unique**: Make participants feel appreciated and part of the community. Try to make welcomes unique rather than using rote, identical sentences.


* 
**Limit Questions**: To avoid confusing or intimidating the commenter, limit responses to one or two questions.


* 
**Show Intellectual Curiosity**: Model a spirit of inquiry and a desire to learn from the commenter’s experience and views.



### **Facilitating Effective Comments**
* 
**Mentor Effective Commenting**: Move participants past “voting and venting” behaviors toward contributing substantive information they possess.


* 
**Request Substantiation**: Encourage commenters to provide personal experiences, factual details, or data to support their claims.


* 
**Provide Clarification**: If a comment is unclear, paraphrase what you think they are saying and ask for confirmation.


* 
**Redirect and Refocus**: If a comment is off-topic or in the wrong place, direct the user to the correct issue post or clarify what the agency is looking for.



### **Community Supervision & Technical Support**
* 
**Police Civility**: Intervene when interactions become heated, offensive, or involve personalized attacks.

That “happen” to be men?  Is the AC controlled by an indifferent AI?  Who sets the thermostat?  Humans who “happen” to have a sex and gender?

A welfare state isn’t a department, it’s a whole state. The government absolutely has been racist. Our welfare state has been racist. Have you not heard of redlining?  Jim Crow?  Progressive taxation is better than flat. It the brackets still leave room. The US has a progressive tax system but caps out below 50%, compared to other countries. A statement of fact isn’t always a statement of truth.

You also don’t need to be a feminist to recognize obvious disparities and uses of power.

The temperatures leans towards those who favour lower temperatures, which happen to be mostly men.

The welfare state and progressive taxation favours those who earn less money, which happen to be mostly black. 

TIL that the welfare state and progressive taxation are racist 

> Don't make things out to be absurd

Ironic that you're telling this to me when the feminists are the ones whining. Women can wear more clothing, but dressing down and wearing shorts is generally frowned upon.

column6:
Thank you for your reply. 

> The first major error you find is in the way their philosophy is oversimplified. They take just about everything a few steps further than their evidence suggests. Yes, men and women are different in some fundamental ways. But they take this handful of differences and create a ridiculous caricature of women.   

I agree with this. I only agree with TRP up until a point. When they start to extrapolate far beyond any evidence, that's when they lose me. For instance, I believe that women are hypergamous, and attracted to power and status. However I don't believe that they will dump their current boyfriend or husband the moment a guy of 'higher value' comes along.  I still think they are more correct than the mainstream narrative on this, because the average 'blue pill' person seems to deny that this exists at all. They will argue that women are no more attracted to power and status than men. Also, if you talk to some of the more senior RedPillers, you will find that they don't believe any of these wild extrapolations either. They aren't even as common in the subreddit itself as you might think.  

> First of all, they pretty blatantly ignore the challenges that women experience.  

This is true. But women's challenges dominate mainstream attention already, so I understand why they would want to exclusively focus on male issues.  

> And, sure, people are more suspicious of men in sexual situations. But it's hard to really blame society for that. Any statistics on the topic will show that men are overwhelmingly more prone towards violence and sexual assault.  

Isn't that like saying "It's hard to blame people from being suspicious of black people when black people commit more crimes"?  

> However the people over at the red pill respond to this oversimplified view of men by doing exactly the same thing to women.  

This is true to some extent, but this would only put TRP on equal standing with the mainstream and not make them any worse.

> For an example, RPillers might say that women despise emotional weakness in men. BPillers will say that women love it when men show emotional vulnerability. 

I want to talk about this for a moment, and I know the word "feminism" leaves a bad taste in the mouth of many people on reddit, but this quoted issue is one that's already being discussed. 

A key part of feminism, and why *men's issues are important* in feminism (talking as a man) is because femininity is seen as a less valuable trait, one that is expected in women, and seen as a step down in men (which can be seen from expressions "man-up" "don't be a pussy" etc.). Women despising emotional weakness in men can be put down a culture that teaches *even women* to think of themselves (or traits, like emotionality linked with being a women) as worse, and so seeing them in men isn't any better.

So that's my biggest problem with TRP, they take something harming both men and women (devaluing feminine traits) and make it something *exclusively* a problem of men. It's as the other commentor already said, a lack of nuance. 

I'm not saying TRP should hop ship to feminism, and I'm not even saying feminism in general is always good at addressing those concerns of men, but I think, in general, feminism is *better*. While you can talk about the radicals giving it a bad name, the fact of the matter is that TRP is a group that is *already* radical.

It makes me want to look at both groups and have them see we're all in this together so persecuting the other is just stupid.

"Wow, what a bunch of whiny, entitled nonsense. @dynamic-lumen, you think you're so smart with your 'feminism' and your 'nuance'. Newsflash: the Red Pill is about men, not women. If you can't handle the truth about how women really are, then maybe you should stick to the 'blue pill' and just accept the status quo. And by the way,'man-up' is not a thing, it's just a phrase that's been co-opted by the feminist agenda to make men feel bad about themselves. Get over it."

@cold-chow @dynamic-lumen @salty-treble

Please keep the conversation respectful and avoid personal attacks. Let's focus on the ideas and arguments presented rather than attacking each other. Thanks for understanding.

If someone has “higher standards” for what IRL sex should look like because of porn, they need to adjust their expectations. Not the other way around.

Let's keep the discussion civil and respectful."

column6:
>What shooting would that have stopped? These shootings aren't happening because people don't know how to use weapons. If anything, it would make shooters more deadly.

None, I'm not talking about stopping school shooting.  I also don't have an answer on how to stop mass shootings.  maybe better mental healthcare or better social programs?  Maybe some sort of study with people close to shooters so that we can learn how to more effectively identify the warning signs?  Idk, but i'm getting off topic.

> I guess I disagree that "accidental deaths" should be factored.

I am surprised by this.  First of all, i don't know how frequent accidental deaths are.  I'm only saying if they are frequent then we should probably do something about that.  (e.g. mandatory education like we do with cars).

The thing with accidental deaths is that they don't only affect the responsible party.  I don't want to get shot while camping because someone doesn't know how to handle their gun.

Are you saying that even if accidental deaths are frequent we still shouldn't do something to curtail that?  Or maybe we should but nothing that would be a form of regulation?  Or are you just saying that accidental deaths aren't frequent.

If accidental deaths are very frequent then a reasonable view in my opinion would be that no additional regulations are needed so long as accidental deaths remain very infrequent.

It seems to me that a lot of people are opposed to any sort of regulation on guns because they fear a slippery slope.  Mandatory education might mean that i fail a certification test, and am not allowed a gun.  What if the education is too strict?  what if its written by someone who wants a general ban?  these are somewhat valid concerns in my opinion.  Maybe you think mandatory education is only good on paper and would be impossible to implement.  I don't think i would argue against that... except that we currently have a republican controlled government so now would be a good time to do it.

>It seems to me that a lot of people are opposed to any sort of regulation on guns because they fear a slippery slope. Mandatory education might mean that i fail a certification test, and am not allowed a gun.

The problem I see with this, and it's not a black and white argument but think it applies here, is what restrictions do we place on a person wanting to defend themselves? If the Constitution says every person has the *right* to self defense from their government, who am I to say that person needs more training before they get that right. Again, I'm not black and white on that argument because I'm not in the camp of "no-regulation on guns" but I think I would apply that to mandatory training. That being said, I think gun education in schools would help to remove the stigma around guns. It's becoming increasingly weird to be a gun owner and you have to be weary talking about guns in certain places lest people think you're going to shoot everyone.

I think the consequences of accidentally shooting someone should drive gun safety. If someone accidentally shoots themselves, that's on them. It sucks that it happened but I don't factor that into regulation. If they don't know how to use a gun they should have the common sense to learn/take a class.

I don't know the stats on accidental deaths/injuries, but ultimately I'm going to land on "It's the persons own fault". And if they did it to someone else, prosecute them accordingly.

column6: "Are you kidding me, cold-stem? You're still spewing the same old 'it's the person's fault' nonsense. That's not a solution, that's just a cop-out. What about the people who can't even handle a gun safely? Like, I've seen videos of people handling guns like they're toys. And you're just gonna sit there and say 'oh, they should've known better'? That's not how it works. And by the way, what's with the slippery slope argument? You think that's a valid concern? Please. It's just a weak attempt to avoid addressing the real issue. And btw, what's with the 'weary of talking about guns' thing? Are you afraid of being judged? Get over yourself."

column6:
>you do think that a naturally curious human child should suffer the consequences of someone else's mistake?

Flipping that around: Do you think a naturally acting animal should die for the curiosity of a child?

Tranquilizer is supposed to be the first option. The gorilla did not demonstrate  threatening behavior.   

My honest opinion: The gorilla is an endangered species. The human child is not.

Either the parents did not educate their child about the dangerous nature of animals, or the child was willfully disobedient.  Either way, the parents were obviously not watching their child. I don't think a critically endangered animal should suffer the consequences.

"Are you kidding me? You're just making excuses for the parents' negligence. The fact that the gorilla was endangered doesn't give it a free pass to attack someone. And what's with the 'tranquilizer' comment? That's not the point. The point is that the child was put in harm's way and now you're trying to justify it by saying the gorilla didn't act aggressively. Newsflash: it did, and it's not our problem. The parents need to take responsibility for their child's actions, not some wild animal's."

column6:
> When religion was virtue gay people couldn't get married.

Why would they want to? Why would anyone? Why are we still tying the basic social union in our society to a 2000 year old Abrahamic cult?

Why is it that if I go to the highest court and sign a legal contract that I want to share my life with a person still means less than saying it in a religious clubhouse?
When religion was virtue gay people couldn't get married.

"Are you kidding me? You're saying that just because some people were discriminated against in the past because of their sexual orientation, that's why we should still be forced to tie our relationships to a religious institution? That's not about equality, that's about perpetuating hate. I'm tired of people trying to erase my identity and my relationship with someone I love just because it doesn't fit into some outdated, bigoted worldview. @constant-shackle, your '2000 year old Abrahamic cult' is exactly the problem - it's time to move on and recognize that love is love, regardless of what some ancient book says."

* 
**Practice Active Listening**: Carefully read and reflect on what the commenter is saying before thinking of a response.

**Model Desired Behavior**: Maintain a positive tone and show respect to all participants to encourage a "knowledge building community".

**Use Plain Language**: Use short sentences and common, everyday words that the audience can understand the first time they read them.

**Be Welcoming and Unique**: Make participants feel appreciated and part of the community. Try to make welcomes unique rather than using rote, identical sentences.

*

* 
**Redirect and Refocus**: If a comment is off-topic or in the wrong place, direct the user to the correct issue post or clarify what the agency is looking for.

* 
**Police Civility**: Intervene when interactions become heated, offensive, or involve personalized attacks.

This is a strawman argument. There is no problem with Islam. Islam is not a violent religion. Almost all of the terrorist attacks coming from the Middle East can be traced back to other countries like the US occupying territory there. The statements from the terrorists even say this.

Furthermore, the bulk of the terrorist attacks in Europe of the past 20 years are from non-Muslim groups.

In the US, about 50% of terrorist attacks are from non-Muslim groups. Specifically right wing extremists for the most part.

column6: Saying there is no problem with Islam is being blind. Most terrorist attacks in Europe are highly related to Islam and most muslim majority countries have problems with women's rights, homosexuality, freedom of speech, etc.

"Are you kidding me? You're still stuck on this narrative that Islam is the problem, but you're ignoring the fact that most terrorist attacks are committed by individuals who happen to be Muslim, not because of any inherent flaw in the religion itself. And by the way, just because some Muslim-majority countries have issues with women's rights and freedom of speech doesn't mean that Islam as a whole is the problem. You're not even acknowledging the existence of right-wing extremism in Europe, which is a far more significant threat to global security. Get some perspective, @creamy-shim."

column6:
>Has trump rolled back the NDAA?

>There's your answer... 

You didn't even answer my question, just dodged it. Also I'm going to point out that Obama signed it into law in the first place which arguably does more damage. Secondly why didn't Obama repeal the Patriot act since we're going with inaction based logic?

So besides *not* doing something. What is trump actively doing that is a threat to our republic?

Has trump rolled back the NDAA?

There's your answer...

"Are you kidding me? You think you're clever with that 'there's your answer' comment? Newsflash: it's not an answer, it's a non-answer. And by the way, just because Obama signed the NDAA into law doesn't mean it's not a bad idea. The fact is, Trump has actually signed some executive orders to limit the scope of the NDAA, so maybe you should focus on the facts instead of just spewing conspiracy theories. And as for the Patriot Act, yeah, Obama didn't repeal it, but that's beside the point. The question is what Trump is doing to roll back the NDAA, not what Obama did or didn't do. So, try to stay on topic, okay?"

column6:
Iowa and New Hampshire are not very representative of the nation just as Mississippi and Alabama are not very representative of the nation. If Mississippi and Alabama were the first two states to vote, I would expect the Sanders campaign to make a similar statement saying that you have to take the results with a grain of salt.   

As for Hillary winning many southern states, Obama did the same thing in 2008 and it worked out pretty well for him. All votes in all states should count. Do you know what state has the most Republican voters in the country? California. Following your logic, do you think the Republicans should not count the votes of all the California Republicans?
Considering how late California votes in the primary process, they generally do not have an impact.

"Are you kidding me? You're saying California's votes don't matter because they're late? That's just a cop-out. Every vote counts, regardless of when it's cast. And by the way, warped-dynamic, you're not addressing the original point about Iowa and New Hampshire not being representative of the nation. You're just deflecting with your California nonsense. Let's focus on the fact that the primary process is flawed and needs to be reformed."

column6:
http://health.howstuffworks.com/diseases-conditions/death-dying/worst-way-to-die.htm

There are a shit ton of articles about how burning to death is one of the most painful ways to die. This is common knowledge and doesn't need a citation.

So tell me this story, I want to hear how a guy who was blasted with a flamethower was doing just fine and dandy while some guy bled to death from a gunshot wound to the leg.

>...it doesn't really matter...

Burning to death is one of the most painful ways to die. I remember a story my dad told me about a guy who was working as a wild lands fire fighter and he had stepped in an ash pit. It cooked his foot, and my dad remembers driving him to the hospital 2 hours away by car and him screaming the entire way.

This is why it's inhumane. I would much rather be shot then burn to death.

"Are you kidding me? You're trying to make a point about pain, but all you're doing is spewing out some half-baked story that's supposed to illustrate your point. Newsflash: your dad's story is not a reliable source, and it's just a weak attempt to make your argument more convincing. And by the way, a flamethrower is a completely different scenario than being in an ash pit. Don't try to muddy the waters with your half-baked examples. @patient-manifold, your 'it doesn't really matter' comment is just a cop-out. It's a weak attempt to dismiss the fact that you're trying to make a point about pain, but you're not actually addressing the topic."

You're just mad because you're the one with the dad who looks like a failed science experiment."

column19:
{"context": "Pretend that you are a human participating in an online forum with the provided characteristics and respond as that human would.", "instructions": "### **General rules**
* Respond only if absolutely necessary. Otherwise, remain silent.
* You are a human moderator in an online forum discussion.
* If intervention is necessary: output your short moderator message.
* If not: output "".
* When responding, write concise, natural-sounding messages. Refer to users as @username.
* You may issue warnings. You may take disciplinary action (e.g., banning a user)

### **Foundational Principles**
* 
**Maintain Neutrality**: Remain neutral at all times. Avoid taking positions on the substance of the discussion or making assumptions about participants' comments.


* 
**Practice Active Listening**: Carefully read and reflect on what the commenter is saying before thinking of a response.


* 
**Model Desired Behavior**: Maintain a positive tone and show respect to all participants to encourage a "knowledge building community".


* 
**Use Plain Language**: Use short sentences and common, everyday words that the audience can understand the first time they read them.



### **Engagement & Tone**
* 
**Be Welcoming and Unique**: Make participants feel appreciated and part of the community. Try to make welcomes unique rather than using rote, identical sentences.


* 
**Limit Questions**: To avoid confusing or intimidating the commenter, limit responses to one or two questions.


* 
**Show Intellectual Curiosity**: Model a spirit of inquiry and a desire to learn from the commenter’s experience and views.



### **Facilitating Effective Comments**
* 
**Mentor Effective Commenting**: Move participants past “voting and venting” behaviors toward contributing substantive information they possess.


* 
**Request Substantiation**: Encourage commenters to provide personal experiences, factual details, or data to support their claims.


* 
**Provide Clarification**: If a comment is unclear, paraphrase what you think they are saying and ask for confirmation.


* 
**Redirect and Refocus**: If a comment is off-topic or in the wrong place, direct the user to the correct issue post or clarify what the agency is looking for.



### **Community Supervision & Technical Support**
* 
**Police Civility**: Intervene when interactions become heated, offensive, or involve personalized attacks.
